expert_commentary: In this example, the number of effective moves made by Red are three moves. Although the Red cannon had gained a pawn, it was forced to retreat to avoid capture by the Black horse and is considered to have made two moves, as theoretically speaking, Red could have only played C2+2 followed by C2=5 to reach its current position.||||Black, on the other hand, would have made four effective moves, one each for the cannon, horse, advisor and chariot. ||Black would now have the initiative in hand and also better development of the chariot as the Red chariot on the right flank CANNOT be developed with R1=2 now. ||This is one of the concepts that the player with a background in International Chess must apperciate

Central Cannon 中炮/当头炮||Orthodox Opening.||Most commonly played Red opening since the ancient times. Accounts for about half of all Xiangqi games.||By default, C2=5 is given, probably due to the fact that most people are right handed and would be more prone to move the cannon on the right
Same Direction Cannons. 顺炮/顺手炮||Black would move the cannon on the ipsilateral side of the board as the central cannon
Same Direction Cannons. 顺炮/顺手炮||Black would move the cannon on the ipsilateral side of the board as the central cannon

At this point in time, Red would choose to develop his chariot as a filed chariot or ranked chariot. Some newer variations include deferring the development of the right chariot and choosing to play P3+1 or H8+7
Filed Chariot

Central Cannon 中炮/当头炮||Orthodox Opening.||Most commonly played Red opening since the ancient times. Accounts for about half of all Xianqi games.||By default, C2=5 is given, probably due to the fact that most people are right handed and would be more prone to move the cannon on the right
Opposite Direction Cannons. 列炮/逆手炮||Traditional ODC. Black would counter with the central cannon for the first move

The elephant moves are not calculated as red" width="500" height="350" frameborder="0" marginwidth="0" marginheight="0" scrolling="no">   The above is a simple guide to evaluate your opening. But it has its pitfalls too. It does not take into consideration the positioning of the pieces. For example, a well positioned cannon may require the opponent to make a few moves before escaping its threat. Also, it does not take into consideration the value of the pieces. For example, sometimes a chariot is exchanged for a horse and cannon. The chariot might have moved only say x moves but the horse and cannon might have made y moves where y>x. Efficiency is gained by the chariot’s side but the value might not be worth it. The third point to note is better explained with a scenario. Let’s say a pawn has crossed the river and made a few moves. The effective moves for the pawn would be say z moves. However, that initiative is readily erased should the pawn be captured, like in the case of the horse in example 2. So, another important principle in your opening is the ability of your pieces to remain unscathed after making many moves. Note that the above system is generally useful only in openings, ie roughly the first 10-20 moves of the game. Once the game enters mid-game, it becomes useless, as multiple exchanges have already occurred. Positioning of the pieces followed by material advantage becomes key. I was taught this concept when I attended a Xiangqi introductory lesson by GM Hu Ronghua early in the 1990’s when he visited Singapore. The way GM Hu presented the concept opened a whole new world in Xiangqi for me. I still recall GM Hu stressing the importance of trying to make all your moves as 100% (although impossible) efficient as possible. Since then, my buddies and me have coined it the 100% theory. A few months ago, I read about this concept again in an introductory book when I prepared to create this website. It has helped me much and I hope it helps you with your game too. Reference: 1.
